speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mrs. Fela Abramowicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what happened. Then, on the eve of liberation, in the morning, first thing in the morning, 11 people-- we had still to lose some people-- who lived through all those agonies, and had to die. We buried them in the forest. And it was very sad. We left with very much sadness. We left the partisan. It was an incredible feeling. They was-- there were a few wagons, but most people were walking. A whole row of people walking. We went through dead animals and people with their guts out-- soldiers. Mostly German soldiers. I couldn't look at it, but it was sort of a good feeling, to see them suffer a bit. And through all these things. And before we came out, all walking, walking. I think we walked a few days. We were walking towards Nowogrodek. We stopped over in villages, and they gave us some refreshments. Because I couldn't imagine all those people-- how many of us was still left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still-- is that-- you are being killed, and still so many of us left. That's how they talked to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They thought that that's too many of us still left. Some people. They couldn't hold up their wonderment. They said-- oh, so many of us still left-- of you still left, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, well, we didn't expect much sympathy from-- from them. At this time, we were very bitter and disillusioned with the-- with the local people. Of course, there were a few wonderful people who helped us. But too few.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking, and finally we arrived in Nowogrodek. Where we reassembled again, on a-- on a place in Nowogrodek. And the komandir told-- said farewell to us all. He said, now we are no longer Otriad-- you can go your own ways. And everybody do what they like. He divided-- he gave-- he gave us a cow and a horse to my husband. And we went, and my husband found a house-- his house was burnt out. He still had the land, but we couldn't build anything on the land. And so he asked his brother-in-law-- or his brother had a house somewhere in Nowogrodek-- nice house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a few more couples. I think there were six rooms, so six couples or six families. Nobody even was thinking about having-- living in-- even one room was too much. I felt lonely, you know? I said-- we didn't have anything. The walls were empty. Just made a bed or something. So we had a-- we were used to living in groups, you know, not living by themselves. So I think a few families-- more or less people who knew each other-- who ended up in this house. And-- and start looking for-- you know, start normal life, if it was possible. How long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted to take my husband to the army, about two days later. And, you know, he stalked it off somehow, and got him a job into the trade makers as a saddle-maker, because they needed this for the war effort. So that gave him a little time-- respite-- from joining the army. But every time, you have to think of something else, because they were running out of men. And we stayed in Nowogrodek till we found out that the Nowogrodeks going to-- after they-- the-- there was-- finalize the borders-- that Nowogrodek-- this part of Poland's not going to be Poland anymore. It's going to belong to White Russia-- Belarus-- to the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, since the Soviet Union was still, in '44-- in the year '44-- allied with America and the West, so, as I said, the Polish-- and there was a Polish government already established in Poland, in a certain part of Poland, which was free-- like, in Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I said, the Polish citizens who prefer to be Polish citizens rather than Soviet citizens have the right to repatriation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took this opportunity to go to Poland. Which wasn't yet established, and there wasn't an army. And we can see what we can do. Because there, he had to go to the army, and everything-- everything was finishing. Everybody who went to the army didn't return, because they send them right to the first front-- first fire and all of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the partisans that came out of the-- of the forests, that survived all the Holocaust and everything, and went to the armies-- they were all killed. One of our-- of our group was killed who came-- who escaped with us, left a son. And Asael got killed. He went into the army. He got killed in a battle right after the liberation, one of the Bielski brothers. They were all--thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to somehow-- by-- by our wits, we tried to get out of getting-- going into the army. So we choose this loophole, and went to the thing-- to the-- whatever the authority was they established for this-- for this district-- the high authority. And we said, we are Polish citizens, and we want to stay Polish citizens. And we want to be repatriated. So we did it-- got accepted. The reason was, you know, they couldn't say anything else, because they would then, you know, break the promise to the-- to America and to the Allies. So they let us get out of Nowogrodek. So in, let's say, when was it? Beginning of December. 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: We set out on our journey to Poland. And after we did this-- we were the first one to do it-- a few more people followed. They found out from us-- because we had some inside information-- the followed us, and so a lot of people took this way out, and they all signed for repatriation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where some would have-- I think a wagon-- a-- a goods-- a-- what do you call it? A goods wagon-- train. And we put in-- in inside that stove, and put a few beds, or something. Mattresses filled with straw, to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in the middle of a very severe winter. And we went-- came out on our journey to Poland. And it lasted about three weeks. In which we starved. We-- we were nearly frozen. We took provision with us, but we couldn't cook anything. We had dry stuff, and just longing for a bit of-- And it was very cold. We didn't have any fuel. So one night a couple of men went out and they-- they took-- a fell a-- a telegraph pole-- and chopped it down. If they were caught, they would have been shot. But it had to be this, or freeze. They did this. And we spent three weeks, and we didn't-- [INAUDIBLE] on the way, we learned that Warsaw was liberated, that, and, you know, the Germans were pushed to their own territories. So that was good news. And we-- we-- by this time, the capital of the part of Poland which was liberated was Lublin. So we were heading for Lublin. We had papers-- everything. And we got stuck in a town before Lublin, I think-- I think 30 kilometers or something, or 60 kilometers. I can't remember [? now ?]. In a place called-- in a town called Chelm. And we slept over there. We couldn't get into Lublin, because it was-- the lines were broken. Right through. You had to wait, stop, and wait for the lines to be repaired, and stop. And that's why it lasted so long. Because the lines were-- they were bombed [? or ?] destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no time to-- you know, we were on our last, really, leg, as they say. And the men had a-- couple of men had a talk among themselves and said, let's go have a look at what's going on in Chelm. Maybe we can stop over here, instead of going to Lublin. So they got down to Chelm, and they found a few rooms. And I got a little room. Paid some money to the thing, to get in-- some key money. And we left the train. And we settled down in Chelm. Did you make any attempt to contact your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody to contact to. [? SS ?]-- Later on, we found out what happened to them. Because up until '41, till-- till the Germans attacked, we had regular correspondence with them in the ghetto. We were sending them parcels of food and other things, and they are sending us letters. There was no problem, because German and the Soviet Union were at this time Allies. So they had to pretend that everything is normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got letters. We send them parcels. We receive letters they have received the parcels. So everything was fine, until they attacked, and later on we just could not. Anyway, we looked for everybody, but nobody turned up. So there was you and your husband and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was me, my husband, my child, and my two sisters. And then I found out that my brother, who was sent to Siberia, survived. He came to Poland later on. I-- so at the first, it was me and my two sisters and my husband and my child. I didn't tell you about my sisters yet. You want-- this is a special chapter. It's interesting, if you want to know. Well, when-- and so this was how we came to-- to Poland. And we start in Chelm, until we departed for the displaced-peoples' camp. They came from-- not Israeli, but Palestine fought with-- [INAUDIBLE] alongside the English, in the Middle East and in Africa and everywhere. And so at this moment they were in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came, and they heard about-- of course, they found out everything what's happening. And there was a-- to rescue all the Jews from-- survived from the Holocaust and bring them to Palestine. So they came to us to Chelm, to Poland, and they said, you-- you want to go, or you want to stay here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, we want to go. Because we made up our mind. The first opportunity we have to leave this place, we're going to the other end of the world. We're not staying here for one minute longer than it's necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they came and told us they want to take us out of Poland, we said, all right, we're going. It wasn't the right thing to do, but at this moment it seems right, because we had to go through very bad-- bad-- pass-- while the others who went later went out just what-- on certain trains, and they came right-- [LAUGHS] so they did the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at this time, it was necessary for a for few reasons. So [? we'll ?]-- so we decided to leave, and we left. But in the meantime, you want to know about my family-- what happened. My sister survived before I escaped. And my husband says he'll come and get them back to the partisan-- it became impossible. Because after they found out that people are escaping, they put more fences-- another wall and thing-- and getting in and out of ghettos become very, very hard. Much harder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were trying even to put the arm-- the rescue to the ghetto-- attack the partisans. But we talked it over at meetings, but nothing came out of it. We thought we-- the odds were too small to attack the ghetto and liberate without everybody getting killed in the process. So they abandoned it. So my sisters were in the ghetto, still. So they were in the ghetto till May-- I think it was May-- till May, '43, when another Aussiedlung took place. Out of the 500 who were left, [INAUDIBLE] a half of them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went in the second aktion.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister stayed, only because the-- the-- in the last minute the-- the [? demands ?]-- man-- the German in charge-- came running and said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- he-- he-- pulled her out, and she pulled out my sister. Because their wives told him not to kill her. Because they wanted their dresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in this way, she was-- she was-- she came to be alive to the last 250 people. And she took my sister-- the other sister-- with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they really decided to escape. The whole ghetto's trying to escape. And they thought of all sorts of plans to escape, to amass sort of whoever save themselves, save them. Whoever not, at least they'll put up a resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister, somehow-- she didn't look Jewish. Both my sisters had blue eyes and blonde hair-- and they were women-- they put scarves over them. And somehow they got out, by some--

speaker: interviewee
text: And people watched them. They helped them get out. Because they knew that-- that, once they get out, they will find us. And they got out, and a few other people after them got out. They watched them go out. The other people got picked up by the partisans and brought to the Otriad. They didn't get picked up anywhere. And the people said they went the wrong way. They must have been picked up by the Germans and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since a week passed, and two weeks, and three weeks, and a month, and two months, and they didn't show up, so I assumed they were killed. You know? There was no other answer to it. So I-- in those days, those things we just accepted, you know? You just mourn it, but you didn't have time to mourn, because you're fighting for your own life. So one day when we came to the Naliboki Pushcha-- when we went to the Naliboki Pushcha, as I described before-- the pushcha was teeming with partisans. And some of our Otriad found some cousins, and someone found some-- some uncles or some-- one found a brother. Didn't know he was alive. And they found him among the other partisans. And I was just thinking-- maybe something like this could happen to me? After all, I didn't-- they escaped. I better-- this was just a thought-- I abandoned this-- this thought, because, well, it's silly to hope, you know? And not possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we went into the pushcha, there was a sort of a-- a path. And we were very tired, and we sat down on this path. And the commander said, you can sit down and rest-- and have a rest. We'll stay here for now, and we'll go and we'll find a place where to make camp. And I was just sitting there. My husband said, I'll go and find something to eat. You know, there was mushrooms or berries-- something-- always something to-- and I was very tired. So I put my little bundle under whatever I had in my-- under my-- my head. And put my head down. And almost felt like falling asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I say-- in the last minute I said, oh, wouldn't it be wonderful if I found-- once again, the-- the thought came to me-- wouldn't it be wonderful if I found somebody who could tell me what happened to my sisters? And I fell asleep. No longer was I asleep two minutes, someone is waking me. And they said, are you Fela Abramowicz? I said yes. There are two partisan-- Russian partisans-- riding through the pushcha, and they have a letter for you. I tell you, that's exactly how it happened. They have a letter for you. I said, a letter for me? From whom? So I quickly rush to the partisans. And they pull out a letter. I said, and this was from my sisters. And I said, how come? How did you get this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, he said I went-- somewhere in a-- in a place, where there's a lot of partisans, they were in a-- since they were women, my sister could sew. So one of the peasants took her in and gave them food. And she did for the-- it was a nice young couple after the marriage. And they gave them food, and they kept them in their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my sister wanted to join the partisans-- my both sisters. So once a partisan came in, and she said-- she said, I've got my sister and my brother-in-law in the Bielski partisan. If you ever meet them, could you give them a letter? It was all by chance. If you ever meet them. So it happened at this particular time, that they happened to ride right through it. Otherwise, I would have never seen this letter. He just had arrived. He sees people, sitting near a pathway, and said, who is that? He said, that's Bielski's Otriad. And they said, oh, Bielski's Otriad? I've got a letter for someone from Bielski's Otriad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was by chance-- by pure chance. And I receive this letter. They tell me what-- what happens [? is a ?]-- that they're alive and well in this-- in this particular place. And so, of course, we start, you know, celebrating. And my husband went-- after we came to our place where we could sort of establish a little camp, temporarily-- my husband went with somebody else and picked them up and brought them to the Otriad. So we were reunited. And that was the story-- yes. That was the story how I got back my sisters. [LAUGHS] Then we were three sisters. And they were with the partisan right to the liberation, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to Chelm, then, where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- how-- what did you do after Chelm? Did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there, trying to make a living. My husband was sort of-- we didn't have any money-- not much. We bought something, you know. In the time that we-- he worked in the factory there, and then he used to go to the-- to the villages and repair people's saddles-- you know, peasants-- everything. We had very little money. We brought some food with us, too-- a lot of food from things. The territories in Poland began to be liberated, one by one. And certain parts of Poland, which were liberated later-- Krakow, in-- in Silesia, you know-- and Katowice. And these places weren't ruined by war. They were intact, and they were-- they had lots of household goods and clothes and everything. But they don't have any food. And we had food, and we didn't have any other household goods or clothes or any other consumer goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband took a friend who was with us. We were living in the same-- we bought two adjoining rooms in Chelm when we lived there. So he took him, and they took suitcases, and they filled the suitcases with food, and they went to those places that were freshly liberated and-- and got some-- exchanged the food for the other goods and brought the goods and sold them to the people who didn't have [LAUGHS] any food.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you did in Russia. Because one place has everything, one place had nothing of thing-- one had something else, and the other one-- that's how the speculate-- the black market existed in the Soviet Union. Because it was very-- not centralized. Everything was sort of-- Nobody knew where-- what-- what was where. One place has too many salt-- too much salt, The other place had no salt at all. So the people with salt came to-- to sell that salt here. They bought something that this place has and sell it to the other place. That's how you made your living, in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was war. Nothing was regular, see? You sold it on the market. It was all-- over it wasn't-- nothing was illegal. You can-- you can buy what you want, you can sell what you want. It wasn't the Soviet Union anymore. This was free enterprise. So we did this for a while. How many months was that? When did we come-- in January, February, March, April, May, June, July. In July. 40.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Chelm. Yeah. Taken by the Brigade-- the-- the Zionist organization. They say they're taking us to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: July '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, sorry-- this was after the liberation. Not long after the liberation. So we stay--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us, and they took us. And they-- we, all of a sudden, had to abandon our identities. We had to throw away our documents. We're not allowed to have any valuables on us, you know. And completely the identity-- it was appointed one leader for a group, and they wrote down the names-- different names. And we're-- we're supposed to be inmates from a recently liberated concentration camp, traveling south as Greeks. And that gave us sort of-- just in case somebody detains us, that's what we are. They have no proof to say otherwise. You see? We must have no documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- whole Europe was traveling. Nobody was-- there was no tickets-- train tickets. You just hopped on a train if there was one. You hopped on a train, you hopped on a bus. You hopped-- if something was going. Otherwise, you walk on foot. Everything was disorganized. People were returning from-- especially the soldiers-- the Russian soldiers who were returning home, from Hungary, from everywhere, from Czechoslovakia. They were robbing, they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of them were robbing, and a lot of them were-- yeah, there was-- how many years? Four years on the front, and they were a bit wild. You have to be careful not to be raped, not to be robbed, you know? But-- in certain places.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this time-- this sort of thing brings out all this-- the-- the victors if they-- the defeated ones and the victors-- what was done to them, they did to others. You know how it is, in those circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not go to Palestine. Actually, they took us south. But they took us-- established us in displaced-peoples' camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first we went through Czechoslovakia. Being close, to-- we went from, like, from Chelm to Lublin. From Lublin, we went to-- to Krakow, to Katowice-- south, all closer to the-- went, and then went to the Czech border-- went to Czechoslovakia-- not long. And then we went to-- from Czechoslovakia, went to Hungary-- yeah, went to Hungary. Yeah, went from Czechoslovakia to Slovakia, you know? I mean, and this time to Bratislava, because that was the junction where you can go to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Budapest. In Budapest, we stayed for three weeks. In a big house-- must have been an abandoned Jewish estate or something. A huge place with huge rooms, with lots of bathrooms and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're sleeping on the floors, mostly, you know? A bit of-- got a-- prepared a few straw mattresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed for three weeks, which was a very nice stay. We went out, and Budapest wasn't like all the other countries-- already had open shops and lots of food and clothes. And it was like-- Although a lot of-- Budapest ended actual the war-- the European war-- the last battles were in Budapest. A lot of houses were still burning. One part of it was absolutely undisturbed. And beautiful shops with beautiful clothes, and markets were full of luscious fruit. And we really gorged ourselves with everything. [LAUGHS] I went out and bought myself a beautiful dress and, for my husband, a suit. The first-- you can call 'civilian clothes,' because before that, we just had, you know, sort of make-- make up sort of-- made up of parachute silk, some blouses, and-- after the thing, some knitwear. You know, what we could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was real stuff, you know? Lovely window shops-- absolutely beautiful. And life and cafes and couples walking. This was, like, normal life. For us, it was something different.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we stayed there for three weeks. The only thing I didn't like in Budapest was the hot food. [LAUGHS] I always got burned. Every time I took a mouthful, I was burned, it was so hot. But they liked hot food. And then they took us to, from Budapest, another place which was close to the Austrian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Brigade-- the Zionist organization organized it all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and they took-- took us from there to a place called Szentgotthard, which was on the border of a-- of a-- of Hungary and Austria. And then we had to cross the Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: On foot. With guides, of course. We had a guide.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to climb right through the Alps, with special-- so-- because, at this time, the Russians were very close. The most-- the worst we feared was get into the Russian hands. Because we were running away from there, you see? And if they catch us and they can identify us, we are lost. So we try to avoid. So this was the only way to avoid, because Austria-- Austria was then divided in four zones. There was the-- you saw there was the Americans, the French, the English, and the Russians. Four quarter-- four-- they had everyone a section of Vienna. [INAUDIBLE]
